title: Context for stable global dependencies

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Context for stable global dependencies; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Context for stable global dependencies
sources.claim: Context can provide stable services such as analytics, API clients, or feature policy to a subtree with an explicit provider boundary.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Context can provide stable services such as analytics, API clients, or feature policy to a subtree with an explicit provider boundary.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A TutorApi interface is provided once and replaced with a fake provider in component tests. Your goal is to design and test a narrow context provider with a documented default and consumer contract. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Remote data and asynchronous UI; javascript browser url; Reducer and context boundary; Component API design. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Context can provide stable services such as analytics, API clients, or feature policy to a subtree with an explicit provider boundary. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Mutable service instances, missing providers, server isolation, and test substitution require deliberate lifecycle design. This draft therefore rejects: Importing a global singleton is always equivalent to a contextual service provider.

includes: Use context for theme, session or service boundaries without placing rapidly changing local state globally.
Design and test a narrow context provider with a documented default and consumer contract.
excludes: Mutable service instances, missing providers, server isolation, and test substitution require deliberate lifecycle design.

outcomes: Design and test a narrow context provider with a documented default and consumer contract.

examples.title: Context for stable global dependencies: concrete case
examples.situation: A TutorApi interface is provided once and replaced with a fake provider in component tests.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A TutorApi interface is provided once and replaced with a fake provider in component tests.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Context makes service dependency and substitution explicit; avoid hidden mutable global singletons.
examples.result: The result follows because Context makes service dependency and substitution explicit; avoid hidden mutable global singletons.
